title: Frankenstein
description: A gothic novel about creation, abandonment, scientific ambition, and social exclusion.
descriptionParagraphs: Frankenstein begins with a scientific act of creation but quickly becomes a story about abandonment. Victor Frankenstein brings a sentient being into existence and then recoils from his responsibility, leaving the creature to learn language, desire, shame, and rage in a world that reads his appearance as monstrosity before it hears his voice.

Shelley's themes include ambition, care, loneliness, revenge, and the ethics of making what one cannot love. The novel endures because it never lets its questions settle into a single lesson about science. It is as much about failed parenthood, social exclusion, and the demand to be recognized as it is about experiment, discovery, or forbidden knowledge.
A gothic novel about creation, abandonment, scientific ambition, and social exclusion.

overview.englishDescription: A gothic novel about creation, abandonment, scientific ambition, and social exclusion.
banningParagraphs: Frankenstein entered censorship debates as a novel associated with science, creation, and outsiderhood. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around morality and political sensitivity.

The earliest event currently captured here is 20th century in South Africa, where Apartheid censors banned circulation. The novel appeared on lists of disallowed books in apartheid South Africa. Canonical fiction could be swept up by broad racial and moral censorship systems.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Everybody's Protest Novel
counterReadings.author: James Baldwin
counterReadings.note: A skeptical response to the politics of moral uplift in canonical anti-slavery fiction.

counterReadings.title: The Wretched of the Earth
counterReadings.author: Frantz Fanon
counterReadings.note: Helps contextualize race, violence, and liberation in books targeted under colonial or apartheid systems.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
